EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation overspread central Pennsylvania from south to north during the morning of December 22, 2022, as a result of a deepening area of low pressure over the Great Lakes combined with a coastal low forming over the Carolinas. The precipitation began as a burst of snow across the area. The snow quickly changed over to rain in the Lower Susquehanna Valley. However, the snow persisted across the central and northern mountains into the afternoon hours where a swath of 3 to 6 inches of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of moderate snow produced 2 to 3 inches of snow across Schuylkill County during the morning and early afternoon of December 22, 2022. I-81 northbound was closed at MM 107 in Frailey Township for over an hour due to a jack knifed tractor trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving area of low pressure passed south of Pennsylvania on March 9, 2022, and progressed off the Delmarva Peninsula and out to sea. This system produced a swatch of 6+ inches of snow in less than 12 hours across portions of the northern mountains of Pennsylvania, with lesser snowfall amounts farther to the south and primarily rain (occasionally mixed with wet snow) in the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting winter storm produced up to 6 inches of snow across northern Clinton County in less than 12 hours on March 12, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front set up over portions of Northeastern Pennsylvania. On the west side, conditions remained rain-free and mostly clear throughout the morning, allowing for the environment to become unstable. A passing cold front later in the day brought showers and thunderstorms, with a few storms becoming severe. Downed trees and wires from damaging wind gusts were most of the reports out of Northeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees.

On the west side, conditions remained rain-free and mostly clear throughout the morning, allowing for the environment to become unstable. A passing cold front later in the day brought showers and thunderstorms, with a few storms becoming severe. Downed trees and wires from damaging wind gusts were most of the reports out of Northeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving area of low pressure passed south of Pennsylvania on March 9, 2022, and progressed off the Delmarva Peninsula and out to sea. This system produced a swatch of 6+ inches of snow in less than 12 hours across portions of the northern mountains of Pennsylvania, with lesser snowfall amounts farther to the south and primarily rain (occasionally mixed with wet snow) in the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting winter storm produced up to 6 inches of snow across Potter County in less than 12 hours on March 12, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper trough moved eastward into New England on July 2, 2022, the eastern half of Pennsylvania remained in the right entrance region of the trough, promoting weak ascent in the atmosphere. A cold front slowly moved southeastward across Pennsylvania, with a prefrontal trough over the lower Susquehanna Valley acting as a primary trigger for afternoon convection. Several discrete and multicell thunderstorms developed within a narrow corridor around the prefrontal trough, in an environment with 2000 J/kg CAPE and up to 1200 J/kg of DCAPE. Deep layer 0-6 km shear of up to 35 kts combined with the instability to promote briefly rotating updrafts with transient supercell characteristics for at least one thunderstorm, which produced ping pong ball size hail. There were also several reports of damaging straight line winds associated with downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter to ping-pong ball sized hail in East Drumore Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to southwestern Chester County during the afternoon and early evening of July 2.  Rainfall totals were as high as 3.5 to 4.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue took place in West Nottingham Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of March 12, 2022. A few rain showers accompanied the front across the area, and then a wave of low pressure developed along the front as it slowly pulled east of central Pennsylvania. As steadier precipitation arrived, colder air arrived behind the front and changed the rain over to snow from northwest to southeast across the area into the mid-morning of March 12. A relatively short-lived period of moderate to heavy snow produced widespread 4 to 6 inches of snow, with localized amounts approaching 10 inches. Gusty northwest winds resulted in considerable blowing snow and reduced visibilities for many locations by the afternoon of March 12, as low pressure continued northeastward and rapidly intensified off the New England coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 5 to 7 inches of snow across Perry County in less than 12 hours on March 12, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance generated a broken band of snow showers that crossed central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of December 17, 2022. These snow showers produced brief periods of moderate snowfall, although generally an inch or less of accumulation was observed. However, this was enough to slicken roads in some spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broken band of snow showers produced a quick inch of snow across portions of Centre County during the early morning hours of December 17, 2022. This short-lived snowfall produced slick conditions on area roads, and PA Route 350 was closed in both directions in Rush Township for a couple of hours due to icy conditions and several disabled vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that produced large hail and high winds developed across the Ohio River Valley on the evening of June 8th. Numerous supercell storms formed hours before reaching eastern Ohio along an advancing warm front; the main threat occurred between 6pm and 10pm. The vicinity of the strongest storms was south of I-70 where the moisture boundary resided. Mid-level dry and strong deep wind shear helped the development of unusually large hail (up to 2 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inch hail reported via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving slowly northward over a moist cool air mass already in place resulted in widespread dense fog across the far northwestern suburbs of Philadelphia, Lehigh Valley, and parts of the Poconos during the early morning hours of December 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of dense fog was observed at Queen City Airport and Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry system brought widespread precipitation to the region, including a coating of snow to the southern Poconos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread coating of 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across Monroe County. The highest totals were near Mount Pocono, with the highest report of 3.8 inches 1 mile north of Mount Pocono.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm brought widespread precipitation to the region. Accumulating snow and some light wintry mixed precipitation fell across portions of eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow totals ranged from around 2 to 3 inches in the lower elevations to 5 to 7 inches across the Pocono Plateau. The highest reported total was 7.4 inches 1 mile north of Mount Pocono. Some light sleet and freezing rain also mixed in at times as precipitation diminished.

The highest reported total was 5.8 inches in Albrightsville. Some light sleet and freezing rain occurred as precipitation diminished and temperatures warmed just above freezing.

The highest reported total was 4.2 inches near Portland. Some light sleet occurred as precipitation diminished and temperatures warmed above freezing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation occurred. A light glaze of freezing rain less than a tenth of an inch occurred across the majority of Berks County, especially at higher elevations. Accumulating snow totals ranged from a dusting to 1 inch across Berks County. The highest reported total was 0.7 inches in Hamburg. The highest snowfall totals occurred near I-78.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of March 12, 2022. A few rain showers accompanied the front across the area, and then a wave of low pressure developed along the front as it slowly pulled east of central Pennsylvania. As steadier precipitation arrived, colder air arrived behind the front and changed the rain over to snow from northwest to southeast across the area into the mid-morning of March 12. A relatively short-lived period of moderate to heavy snow produced widespread 4 to 6 inches of snow, with localized amounts approaching 10 inches. Gusty northwest winds resulted in considerable blowing snow and reduced visibilities for many locations by the afternoon of March 12, as low pressure continued northeastward and rapidly intensified off the New England coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 5 to 7 inches of snow across Schuylkill County in less than 12 hours on March 12, 2022. This snow was accompanied and followed by winds gusting over 40 mph, which caused continued blowing and drifting snow through the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northward from the eastern Carolinas on the 22nd to eastern Pennsylvania on the morning of the 23rd.  It was then absorbed by a stronger low moving across the eastern Great Lakes.  The system produced moderate tidal flooding along the lower Delaware River and its tributaries in southeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of lower Bucks County. The tide gauge at Burlington (Burlington County, New Jersey) reached 10.77 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at Newbold reached 11.39 feet MLLW.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal areas of Philadelphia.  The tide gauge at the US Coast Guard Station reached 9.49 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at Bridesburg reached 9.77 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept through the region with light precipitation lingering for a brief period in its wake. Temperatures plummeted below freezing before paved surfaces dried out. This resulted in icy areas on untreated surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lingering light precipitation in the wake of a strong Arctic cold front resulted in a flash freeze of untreated surfaces and icy roads as temperatures plummeted well below freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front set up over portions of Northeastern Pennsylvania. On the west side, conditions remained rain-free and mostly clear throughout the morning, allowing for the environment to become unstable. A passing cold front later in the day brought showers and thunderstorms, with a few storms becoming severe. Downed trees and wires from damaging wind gusts were most of the reports out of Northeastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires down in Rush Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north-south stationary boundary bisected central Pennsylvania ahead of a cold front approaching from the Great Lakes on June 16, 2022. A broken line of showers and thunderstorms formed along a lake-breeze front that developed over northwestern Pennsylvania, just ahead of the approaching cold front. As this line progressed southeastward, one of the cells within the line became a long-track supercell that produced a swath of wind damage and hail from Warren County southeastward to the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees onto wires along Route 555 east of Benezette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary south of the Mason-Dixon line returned northward as a warm front as low pressure pushed up the Ohio Valley during the evening of June 8, 2022. This system generated widespread showers and some strong thunderstorms across southwestern Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and western Maryland. Many of these storms were rotating in the vicinity of the warm front, and some produced sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated between 75 and 85 mph produced wind damage in the Eagle Ridge housing development. Multiple homes in the Eagle Ridge housing development sustained minor structural damage ranging from sheds/outbuildings to roofs (shingles or fallen tree branches) and siding/downspouts. One residence received moderate damage due to a collapsed detached garage which also impacted vehicles that were parked underneath the garage and nearby. Several trees were also uprooted or knocked down. No injuries were reported. The damage was determined by an EMA/NWS coordinated damage assessment to have been straight-line in nature.

Ten injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A somewhat unusual convective environment developed on June 1. A wedge of warm, unstable air existed in between a backdoor cold front dropping from northeast to southwest and another cold front moving in from the west. This shrinking area of warm air was characterized by moderate to strong instability but only weak to moderate shear. A few areas of convection developed while conditions remained favorable, which produced localized instances of damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail report via social media. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain to parts of southeastern Pennsylvania during the early hours of June 9.  Rainfall amounts as high as 1.5 to 2.5 inches fell in a short amount of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rail service was disrupted in Darby due to high water on the tracks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The surface synoptic set-up on June 22, 2022 was similar to that of the severe weather event just 6 days earlier, as a north-south stationary boundary bisected central Pennsylvania ahead of a cold front approaching from the Great Lakes. To the west of the north-south stationary boundary, the airmass over western Pennsylvania was very warm and humid with strong instability. Scattered thunderstorms first developed during the early afternoon over the south-central mountains, with additional activity developing farther north over the central mountains and over western Pennsylvania later in the afternoon.  By early evening, the primary focus for convection was found just ahead of the front as it pushed into western Pennsylvania. Damaging winds were the dominant severe weather type, with only one report of marginally severe hail. One thunderstorm brought locally heavy rain to Altoona and Hollidaysburg resulting in minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail east of Boalsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe mesoscale convective systems (MCSs) passed mostly west and southwest of the eastern mid-Atlantic on June 13 and 14. The majority of severe weather associated with these systems remained over the Ohio Valley and Southeast. A couple of areas of convection did make it further east on the morning of June 14. While instability was limited due in part to unfavorable diurnal timing, a few instances of damaging winds occurred over the eastern mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported trees and wires down at the intersection of US-1 and PA-352, which caused a road closure. Time estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated to have been 100-110 mph produced considerable straight-line wind damage between Mifflintown and Thompsontown. The strongest downburst winds associated with the storm occurred at the Centre Lutheran Church, where the roof of the church was peeled off from the north. A metal cross on the church's steeple was also destroyed, while a pine tree adjacent to the church had a piece of its trunk snapped off. The storm then continued|southeastward across the Juniata River, producing tree damage near the PFBC Vandyke Hatchery. Additional tree damage was then found farther southwest along Highway 333 immediately southeast of Mexico and east of Port Royal, associated with the rear flank|downdraft of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that produced large hail and high winds developed across the Ohio River Valley on the evening of June 8th. Numerous supercell storms formed hours before reaching eastern Ohio along an advancing warm front; the main threat occurred between 6pm and 10pm. The vicinity of the strongest storms was south of I-70 where the moisture boundary resided. Mid-level dry and strong deep wind shear helped the development of unusually large hail (up to 2 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey investigated damage in Greene County and noted scattered tree damage along a half mile street of SR 3013 southwest of Oak Grove. Tree limbs and branches were up to 6 inches in diameter, apparent downburst signature.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving area of low pressure passed south of Pennsylvania on March 9, 2022, and progressed off the Delmarva Peninsula and out to sea. This system produced a swatch of 6+ inches of snow in less than 12 hours across portions of the northern mountains of Pennsylvania, with lesser snowfall amounts farther to the south and primarily rain (occasionally mixed with wet snow) in the Lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting winter storm produced up to 6 inches of snow across Cameron County in less than 12 hours on March 12, 2022.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees snapped or uprooted.

Gusty northwest winds resulted in considerable blowing snow and reduced visibilities for many locations by the afternoon of March 12, as low pressure continued northeastward and rapidly intensified off the New England coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 5 to 6 inches of snow across Columbia County in less than 12 hours on March 12, 2022. This snow was accompanied and followed by winds gusting over 40 mph, which caused continued blowing and drifting snow through the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms focused near Cleveland became organized (into a squall line) the evening of June 1st and unleashed strong damaging wind north and west of Pittsburgh. The environment was prime for strong storms to occur: mean layer CAPE was near 1500 J/kg at the time of the squall line and effective bulk shear ranged between 30-35kts. Storms lost their intensity as they approached Pittsburgh and a gust front jetted out ahead of the line of convection, approaching an area that lacked robust instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported power lines down near Pulaski.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms that produced damaging winds developed the evening of June 22, 2022. A broken line of showers and storms developed between Cleveland and Pittsburgh just before 3pm and continue to advances southeast along the axis of a trough entering from the north. Dry conditions under ridging hours ahead of the showers and storms helped prime the environment. Mean level CAPE before convective was between 3000-3500J/kg, and steep lapse rates near the surface (7.5 to 8.0C/km) help with strong damaging updrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down in East Allegheny.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls developed during the day on March 28, 2022 as unseasonably cold air aloft moved over central Pennsylvania. Surface temperatures were anomalously cold as well, with many locations across central Pennsylvania experiencing record cold high temperatures. Snow squalls developed quickly during the morning hours and continued through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. Snow squalls impacted Snyder County with 1/4 mile or less visibility and wind gusts to 30 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall passed over I-81 in Schuylkill County shortly after 10:30am (EDT) on March 28, 2022, causing an 80 vehicle pileup at mile marker 116 on the northbound lanes that resulted in 6 fatalities, an additional 24 injuries, and closed the northbound lanes between mile markers 107 and 119 for more than 24 hours. State Route 901 was also closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving pulse storms created flash flooding conditions across the region. The storms developed in a very moist, buoyant environment where 2000-3000 J/kg SBCAPE and 1.7-1.9 inch PW values, supported very efficient rainfall rates. Shear profiles were weak from the low levels to 300mb and created a 'pulse-type' behavior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported by the beer distributer and surrounding businesses on Sand Hill Rd by Route 30. A pick up truck was reported trapped by water and a water rescue occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The leading edge of a trough initiated convection across the region during the early afternoon of August 29th. With little deep-layer wind shear available for quick storm motion, flash flooding occurred under warm, moist conditions (PWATs ranging 1.8 to 2.0 inches under prolonged southwest flow). A few storms were considered severe, knocking down trees, as updrafts escalated into cool, dry conditions aloft and created strong downdrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused multiple streets in Monaca proper to flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of a mesoscale convective system created a few supercells in eastern Ohio that advanced into northern West Virginia/western Pennsylvania. Two tornadoes spun up from a single supercell. Despite meager instability, deep-layer wind shear was favorable at near 40 knots, and the storm tracked in a favorable direction to ingest rotating updrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 4 large trees down along Pikes Peak Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection ignited along a weak surface boundary during the afternoon of August 4th in northern Ohio and northwestern PA. Westerly deep-layer shear was weak and storms were disorganized. However, MLCAPE increased to 1500-2000 J/kg and low-level lapse rates steepened with time; isolated cells became capable of producing isolated wind damage through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees down across Rockland Cranberry Road.

A narrow band of gusty rain showers accompanied the front, with very little lightning observed. This line of convective showers (and isolated thunder) produced sporadic wind damage as it pushed eastward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a tree onto New Valley Road in Rye Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of March 19, 2022. A line of showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front in a high shear, low CAPE environment. The strongest segment of this line produced wind damage in Schuylkill County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires on Shamokin Street west of Lavelle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration, multi-faceted winter storm hit northwestern Pennsylvania just before Christmas 2022 with extreme cold, strong winds, accumulating snow and extensive blowing and drifting of the snow that fell. An Arctic cold front swept east across northwestern Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of December 23rd. Low pressure along this front tracked over Lake Erie early in the morning on December 23rd and rapidly intensified through the day while slowly lifting northeast across Ontario. With the frontal passage rain changed to snow with temperatures dropping from the upper 30s and lower 40s to sub-zero in about 8 hours. The snow was generally light to moderate, but a brief period of heavier snow with rates of around one inch per hour lifted through during the morning hours December 23rd. The period of snow and rapidly falling temperatures led to a flash freeze and icy conditions on area roadways. While the falling snow became lighter through the afternoon hours of December 23rd, winds began increasing during the morning and gusted over 40 MPH through the day. Peak gusts of up to 55 MPH were measured near the Lake Erie shoreline. Peak gusts across the rest of northwestern Pennsylvania were generally 40-50 MPH. Wind gusts very gradually subsided into December 24th, though gusts didn���t drop below 30mph until that evening. This led to a prolonged period of widespread blowing and drifting snow beginning during the morning December 23rd and continuing through the afternoon and evening, with some patchy blowing and drifting snow continuing overnight and all the way through Christmas Eve. In parts of northwestern Pennsylvania the blowing and drifting snow was extensive enough to lead to blizzard conditions on December 23rd. This storm caused a number of impacts across the area. The greatest impact was to travel due to the flash freeze and extensive blowing and drifting snow, to go along with low visibility. Numerous minor accidents, slide-offs and disabled vehicles were reported throughout the area on December 23rd and 24th. Wind chills dipped to -25 to -40 across all of northwestern Pennsylvania on December 23rd. A number of burst pipes were reported across northwestern Pennsylvania due to this cold, with temperatures remaining in the single digits until Christmas morning. Erie International Airport recorded 3.0��� of snow with this storm, with amounts as high as 8.0��� measured by a trained spotter in Greenfield Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept through the area around 4 AM December 23rd. Low pressure along this front tracked over Lake Erie early in the morning on December 23rd and rapidly intensified through the day while slowly lifting northeast across Ontario. With the frontal passage rain changed to snow with temperatures dropping from the upper 30s and lower 40s to sub-zero in about 8 hours. A period of moderate to heavy snow, with snow rates up to 1��� per hour observed, occurring during the morning and midday hours December 23rd. The period of snow and rapidly falling temperatures led to a flash freeze and icy conditions on area roadways. While the falling snow became lighter through the afternoon hours of December 23rd, winds began increasing during the pre-dawn hours and gusted over 35 MPH through the day. A peak gust of 46 MPH was measured at 1:50 PM December 23rd in Richmond Township. Meadville Airport reported a peak gust of 44 MPH at 7:12 AM. Sporadic tree damage and power outages resulted. Wind gusts very gradually subsided into December 24th, though gusts didn���t drop below 30 MPH until that evening. This led to a prolonged period of widespread blowing and drifting snow beginning early in the morning December 23rd and continuing through the afternoon and evening, with some patchy blowing and drifting snow continuing all the way through Christmas Eve. The greatest impact from this storm was to travel due to the flash freeze and extensive blowing and drifting snow, to go along with low visibility. Numerous minor accidents, slide-offs and disabled vehicles were reported throughout the area on December 23rd and 24th. Wind chills plunged below 0 around 8 AM December 23rd and did not warm above 0 until midday December 26th. A minimum wind chill of -32 degrees was reported at 5:01 PM at Meadville Airport. Weather stations reported minimum wind chills of -32 in Richmond Township and -31 outside of Saegertown. A number of burst pipes were reported across northwestern Pennsylvania due to this cold, with temperatures remaining in the single digits until Christmas morning. The highest reported snowfall with this storm was 4.2���, measured by a trained spotter in Atlantic and by a Co-Op Observer in Springboro. Trained spotters also measured 3.5��� in Meadville and 2.1��� in Townville. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 2.4��� in Conneaut Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of March 12, 2022. A few rain showers accompanied the front across the area, and then a wave of low pressure developed along the front as it slowly pulled east of central Pennsylvania. As steadier precipitation arrived, colder air arrived behind the front and changed the rain over to snow from northwest to southeast across the area into the mid-morning of March 12. A relatively short-lived period of moderate to heavy snow produced widespread 4 to 6 inches of snow, with localized amounts approaching 10 inches. Gusty northwest winds resulted in considerable blowing snow and reduced visibilities for many locations by the afternoon of March 12, as low pressure continued northeastward and rapidly intensified off the New England coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 6 to 7 inches of snow across Clearfield County in less than 12 hours during the early morning hours of March 12, 2022. This snow was accompanied and followed by winds gusting over 40 mph, which caused continued blowing and drifting snow through the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed into an anomalously warm and humid airmass over central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of March 31, 2022, triggering a QLCS that stretched from the New York state line southward into Maryland. CAPE was relatively low (generally <500 J/kg), but shear was high. There were several bowing segments within the QLCS, and there were a number of wind damage reports (and one quarter-sized hail report) that resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph produced multiple reports of trees down a couple miles northeast of Mifflintown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado producing peak winds estimated at 85 to 95 mph tracked from northern Montour County into southeastern Lycoming County during the late afternoon of March 31, 2022. The path length was nearly 9 miles, with a maximum path width of approximately 500 yards. There was one direct injury reported as a result of the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado producing peak winds estimated between 85 to 95 mph tracked from northern Montour County into southeastern Lycoming County. The tornado touched down along Gearhart Road in northern Montour County, knocking down a tree and then causing damage to a barn along Muncy Exchange Road. The tornado continued northeastward into Lycoming County where it caused extensive tree damage along and east of Laidecker Road.|As the tornado moved further into Moreland Township, it knocked down hundreds of trees and damaged a silo roof, barn roof, and porch at various properties along Wallis Road. Dozens of trees were knocked down/snapped off along Moreland Township Road and Church Road and multiple outbuildings were damaged. Damage was observed to trees, barns, and outbuildings on Stahlnecker Road and along Dairy Farm Road toward Lairdsville Rd (PA-118).|Significant damage occurred at the intersection of Old Lairdsville Road and PA-118, where a barn collapsed and was thrown across the yard to the east-northeast. Notably, a piece of lumber was lodged into the hood of a Jeep on the property. Another barn suffered some minor roof damage. The tornado continued northeastward into Franklin Township, knocking down numerous trees along PA-118 into the village of Lairdsville. The most intense area of damage was on the south side of Lairdsville where multiple homes suffered heavy roof damage and broken windows, and barns were damaged. On School Lane, which parallels PA-118 to the northwest, multiple trees were knocked down and another barn was blown apart. Another piece of lumber was thrown through a truck windshield, with glass entering the eye of a passenger and the lumber narrowly missing the other passenger.|East of Lairdsville, a barn was destroyed on Mill Lane. The Lairdsville Butcher Shop experienced some structural damage as well. The tornado continued to produce tree damage along PA-118 northeast of town before lifting about a mile northeast of Lairdsville, with a total path length of nearly nine miles.

Snow showers impacting Somerset County resulted in a jackknifed tractor trailer on Route 56 in Ogle Township that closed the road for a little over one and a half hours.

A narrow band of gusty rain showers accompanied the front, with very little lightning observed. This line of convective showers (and isolated thunder) produced sporadic wind damage as it pushed eastward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires in Middletown and areas to the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system rapidly intensified December 22nd to December 23rd as the center of the low moved from the Midwest across the Great Lakes. A flash freeze, strong wind gusts, and dangerously cold temperatures were recorded across the region. Record low maximum temperatures were broken at several climate sites. Widespread power outages were noted by utility companies, isolated areas experienced internal damage from frozen pipes,  and hazardous travel was noted due to a flash freeze (rain quickly changed to snow) with rapidly dropping temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash freeze created hazardous travel early Friday, December 23th, with a strong cold front. Temperatures dropped from the mid-40s to the single digits between 4:00am and 8am in Allegheny County. Precipitation started as rain and quickly switched to snow with the passage of the front. About an inch of snow fell by 7am on the December 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and slow moving upper-level low centered in the northern Plains impacted portions of the Ohio River Valley with the passage of the warm front on December 15th. Ice accumulated in the ridges of the Laurel Highlands and along our northeastern county warning area with surface temperatures below freezing due to surface winds prevailing from the east. Freezing rain eventually turned to rain as the 50kt low-level jet shifts east into western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolong period of freezing rain on the morning of December 15th in the ridges of Westmoreland County created three tenths of ice to accumulate on surfaces. Highest report was made in Wilpen, PA.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts accompanied a strong low pressure system on December 23rd and continue into December 24th. Measured wind gusts across western PA ranged between 40 to 50mph, sustain winds exceed 30mph. Isolated power outages occurred. A 59mph wind gust was measured near Bellefield, PA in Allegheny County. Allegheny Airport also gusted to 56mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts accompanied a strong low pressure system on December 23rd and continue into December 24th. Measured wind gusts across western PA ranged between 40 to 50mph, sustain winds exceed 30mph. Isolated power outages occurred. 48mph was measured near Tippecanoe along I-80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm moved through Northeastern Pennsylvania on the 15th and 16th. Precipitation started out as freezing mix for some areas and snow for others. Any mix became mostly snow during the day of the 15th. Travel conditions became hazardous, especially during the evening commute, as snow accumulated on roadways. Some locations received a break from the snow overnight before it picked back up on the 16th due to a storm moving north along the Atlantic coast. Snow came to an end early on 17th. While most of the highest snowfall totals were east of I-81, there were locations west that also received significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly moved southeast across northwestern Pennsylvania between the evening of February 2nd and the early morning hours of February 3rd. The front stalled over southwestern Pennsylvania on February 3rd as an even stronger wave of low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley along the front. The slow moving front and waves of low pressure resulted in a prolonged period of wintry precipitation, falling temperatures and gusty winds across the region. A period of more intense snow fell during the afternoon and evening on February 3rd with snowfall rates of up to 1��� per hour observed. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 MPH were observed on February 3rd which led to blowing and drifting snow. The combination of snow, blowing snow, mixed precipitation and falling temperatures led to significant travel impacts across the region with numerous traffic accidents reported across the area. A trained spotter in Harborcreek measured 15��� with this storm. Other select totals include 14.7��� measured by a spotter in Erie, 13.4��� measured by spotters in Cherry Hill and Meadville, and 12.4��� measured by a CoCoRaHS observer in Millcreek Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged winter storm occurred from very early February 3rd through early February 4th, 2022 as multiple waves of low pressure tracked along a slow moving cold front. Rain changed to snow during the early morning hours on February 3rd as temperatures fell below freezing, with a brief period of freezing rain during the transition. Snow continued off and on through all of February 3rd and into the pre-dawn hours of February 4th before finally ending. Snow was steadier and more moderate to heavy during the afternoon and evening hours on February 3rd. Snow rates exceed 1��� per hour at times, especially in the late afternoon and early evening. Wind gusts increased to up to 30 MPH during the afternoon and evening hours of February 3rd leading to some blowing and drifting snow. The snow, blowing snow, and falling temperatures led to a prolonged period of poor travel conditions with auto accidents resulting. The highest reported snowfall came from Meadville, where a trained spotter measured 13.4��� of snow. Spotters also measured 19��� in Atlantic and 7.8��� in Cochranton. Co-Op observers reported 7.8��� of snow in Springboro and 7��� of snow in Titusville with this storm.

A combination of ice, snow, falling temperatures and wind gusts up to 40 MPH led to poor travel conditions. Trained spotters measured up to 7��� of snow in Green Township with this storm, with 5.6��� falling in West Springfield. Ice accumulations of around 0.10��� were common.

Snow continued steadily through much of the night before tapering to more intermittent lake effect snow showers through the morning hours of February 18th. Winds gusted up to 30 MPH at times. Ice accumulations around 0.10��� occurred. Poor travel conditions and auto accidents were reported.
